notable.notable_blurb: Busy country physician of Kensington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: He rode to patients across Kensington parish
narrative_blocks.body: For nineteen years Dr. Percival kept one of the busiest country practices in central Connecticut, making his calls on horseback except in deep winter. A contemporary said few country physicians ever did more business in so short a time.

narrative_blocks.header: He died suddenly at forty in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Percival died suddenly in January 1807, and his daughter Harriet within the same month. The double loss deeply marked his eleven-year-old son, the future poet James Gates Percival.

notable.notable_blurb: Busy country physician of Kensington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: He rode to patients across Kensington parish
narrative_blocks.body: For nineteen years Dr. Percival kept one of the busiest country practices in central Connecticut, making his calls on horseback except in deep winter. A contemporary said few country physicians ever did more business in so short a time.

narrative_blocks.header: He died suddenly at forty in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Percival died suddenly in January 1807, and his daughter Harriet within the same month. The double loss deeply marked his eleven-year-old son, the future poet James Gates Percival.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of poet-geologist James Gates Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart Percival's son James Gates Percival (born September 15, 1795) became a major American poet and the official state geologist of both Connecticut and Wisconsin after his Yale 1815 graduation. Elizabeth herself was described as 'by nature of a susceptible and delicate organization' who 'seems to have imparted to her son these qualities, with a tendency to excessive mental development.' She was understood to have transmitted depression and extreme sensitivity to her sons — James also suffered from depression throughout his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost husband and daughter within five weeks, 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth's first husband Dr. James Percival Jr. died January 22, 1807 in Kensington Connecticut at age forty, and five weeks later her daughter Harriet died February 16, 1807 at age seventeen. The compressed loss of husband and eldest child within five weeks marked the family's catastrophic early-1807. Elizabeth waited five years before remarrying — to Samuel Porter on April 6, 1812.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James worked Porter farm before re-entering Yale
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1812 second marriage to Samuel Porter, Elizabeth's son James Gates Percival worked on Porter's farm before re-entering Yale to complete his 1815 degree. The interim farm labor between the family rebuild and resumption of Yale studies was the kind of practical stop-gap that interrupted many early-Republic professional-track educations after parental deaths reset family finances.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Kensington 1769, four children with Dr. Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart was born in 1769 in Kensington Connecticut (baptized December 24, 1769) and married Dr. James Percival Jr. there in January 1790 at age twenty. Four children survived to be documented: Harriet (died 1807 age 17), Edwin (died Troy NY), James Gates Percival (Yale 1815 poet-geologist), and Oswin Hart Percival (baptized 1809, died never married at a New Haven hospital). Elizabeth herself died around 1846.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT (41.62805, -72.76927) per FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.header: His memory shaped his brother’s biography
narrative_blocks.body: When Julius Ward wrote the 1866 life of the poet James Gates Percival, he leaned on Oswin’s recollections — including that James had tried to overdose on opium in 1820. Oswin was supplying memories gathered fifty years after the fact.

narrative_blocks.header: A boarding-school exile after his father died
narrative_blocks.body: When their father died in 1807, he was sent with his brother James to their uncle Rev. Seth Hart’s school at Hempstead on Long Island. He never married, and died around 1871 in the New Haven hospital; a painter who knew the brothers said they “never seemed to belong to this world.”

notable.notable_blurb: Portrait painter of his Hooker-descended kin

cross_connections.display_label: Painted his uncle and cousin — all three Hooker descendants
Painted his cousin in 1828 — both Hooker descendants

narrative_blocks.header: He painted his own Hooker-descended kin
narrative_blocks.body: A portrait painter of the Hart family of Kensington, Percival painted his uncle Rev. Seth Hart and his aunt Elizabeth around 1828 and 1829. Both sitter and painter descended from Thomas Hooker, a nephew rendering his uncle.

narrative_blocks.header: His brother was a famous poet-geologist
narrative_blocks.body: Edwin was a brother of James Gates Percival, the noted poet and state geologist of Connecticut. The Percivals of Kensington were a remarkably bookish and artistic family.

sources.short: Yale Archives — James Gates Percival Papers

notable.notable_blurb: America's foremost 1820s poet; first Connecticut geologist

narrative_blocks.header: Seven years mapping Connecticut at two-mile intervals
narrative_blocks.body: From 1835 to 1842 he conducted Connecticut's first state geological survey, traversing the state at two-mile intervals over seven years. The survey's accuracy is still cited by earth scientists; he was appointed Wisconsin state geologist in 1854 until his death two years later.

His 1821 'Prometheus' and 1822 'Clio' established him as the most recognized American poet of the 1820s. He was a key figure in early American Romantic literature before turning to lexicography and geology.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited Noah Webster's American Dictionary in 1828
narrative_blocks.body: Noah Webster recruited him in 1827 to edit the American Dictionary of the English Language. The 1828 first edition — Webster's foundational work — owed much of its etymological precision to Percival's editorial labor.

narrative_blocks.header: Entered Yale at sixteen, graduated head of class
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale College at sixteen and graduated at the head of his class four years later in 1815. He earned a medical degree from Yale Medical Institution in 1820 and briefly practiced surgery before turning to writing.

narrative_blocks.header: Suicide attempts and the long poem 'The Suicide'
narrative_blocks.body: In 1820 he suffered a severe mental health crisis after his father's death, making multiple attempts on his own life. He survived, withdrew from society, and channeled his suffering into 'The Suicide' — a long, intense poem from that period.

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle's boarding school — where he first wrote poetry at 14
cross_connections.description: Rev. Seth Hart ran the Hempstead boarding school where Percival and his brother Edwin were sent after their father's death in 1807. Percival was 11-12; Seth was 'amiable, cheerful, almost jovial' — a personality in sharp contrast to his melancholy nephew. Seth tried to get Percival to break out of his shell. According to later accounts, Percival resented this deeply, and directed verses of 'The Suicide' toward his uncle. The relationship was formative and fraught — Seth Hart was the first to witness Percival's literary voice emerge, but also the person Percival blamed for his suffering.

cross_connections.display_label: She asked him to edit her atlas; acknowledged they were cousins
cross_connections.description: Emma Willard and James Gates Percival were second cousins once removed — both 6th-generation Hooker descendants through separate branches of the Hart family, neither knowing of the shared Hooker ancestry. They corresponded 1829-1833: Emma asked Percival to review her Woodbridge & Willard geography textbook in 1829. In 1832 she wrote: 'we are distant relations, your mother having been of the same name and stock as my father... I knew and loved your father — he was our family physician.' Letters held at Yale Beinecke, YCAL MSS 703.

cross_connections.display_label: mapped as geologist the mine Hart secured for 1775
cross_connections.description: The Kensington lead mine on the Mill River — co-owned by Percival's father, half a mile from his childhood home — was managed during the Revolution by Gen. Selah Hart, appointed by the General Court in 1775 to secure lead ore for the colony. Percival surveyed the same mine as Connecticut state geologist, finding galena ore. Two Hooker descendants, two centuries, one mine. (CT Historical Society; Berlin-Peck Memorial Library)

cross_connections.display_label: Both edited Webster's Dictionary — Percival in 1826, Porter in 1865
cross_connections.description: James Gates Percival was effectively the principal editor of Noah Webster's American Dictionary of the English Language in 1826-1828 — 'feeling like the living tied to the dead,' he wrote of Webster. Rev. Noah Porter III edited later editions (1865). Both were connected through Farmington's Hooker-Cowles network: Percival through his mother Elizabeth Hart, granddaughter of Matthew Hart Jr. × Sarah Hooker; Porter through his father's 60-year Farmington ministry and the Cowles family.

Percival was hired by Noah Webster as one of the editors of the 1828 American Dictionary of the English Language and its 1841 revision. Percival's exacting standards were a significant factor in the dictionary's reputation for scholarly accuracy. Both men were Connecticut-born; Webster (West Hartford 1758) was an orbit figure to the Hooker tree; Percival (Berlin CT 1795) descended from Thomas Hooker through six generations.

cross_connections.display_label: Governor Edwards appointed him state geologist 1835

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Yale 1815 at the head of his class; later earned M.D. at Yale.
Earned M.D. at Yale; his medical examination was legendarily rigorous.
